facts.title: The Derivative
facts.summary: The derivative $f'(x)$ is the instantaneous rate of change of $f$ — the slope of the tangent line to the curve at $x$.

detail.body: Average rate of change is the slope of a secant line between two points. The derivative is the slope you get as those two points squeeze together into one.

facts.title: Derivative of a Constant
facts.summary: A constant never changes, so its rate of change is zero.

detail.body: A constant function $f(x) = c$ graphs as a flat horizontal line, and a flat line has slope $0$ everywhere — so its derivative is $0$.

facts.title: Limit Definition of the Derivative
facts.summary: The derivative is the limit of the secant slope as the two points come together.

detail.body: The fraction $\frac{f(x+h) - f(x)}{h}$ is the slope of the secant line through $x$ and $x+h$. As $h \to 0$ that secant rotates into the tangent, and its slope becomes the derivative.

facts.title: Power Rule
facts.summary: Bring the exponent down in front, then reduce the exponent by 1.

detail.body: Any coefficient $a$ rides along, multiplied by the exponent. This shortcut differentiates any power of $x$ instantly — no limit required.

facts.title: Constant Multiple & Sum Rules
facts.summary: Differentiate a polynomial one term at a time; constant multipliers factor out.

detail.body: Derivatives split across addition and subtraction, and constant multipliers come along for the ride. So you can differentiate each term of a polynomial separately and add the results.

facts.title: Sign of the Derivative
facts.summary: The sign of $f'$ tells you whether $f$ is rising or falling.

detail.body: Where $f'(x) > 0$ the curve slopes up; where $f'(x) < 0$ it slopes down; and where $f'(x) = 0$ the tangent is momentarily flat.

facts.title: Critical Points & the First-Derivative Test
facts.summary: Local maxima and minima occur at critical points, where $f'(x) = 0$.

detail.body: At a critical point the tangent is flat. If $f'$ changes from $+$ to $-$ there, it's a local maximum; from $-$ to $+$, a local minimum.

facts.title: The Integral as Area
facts.summary: A definite integral measures the accumulated (signed) area between a curve and the x-axis from $a$ to $b$.

detail.body: Just as the derivative is about slope, the integral is about area. Area above the x-axis counts as positive and area below as negative.

facts.title: Definite Integral as a Limit of Riemann Sums
facts.summary: Slice the region into $n$ rectangles; as $n \to \infty$ the total area becomes exact.

detail.body: Each rectangle has area $f(x_i)\,\Delta x$. Adding more, thinner rectangles drives the approximation toward the true area — that limit is the definite integral.

facts.title: Fundamental Theorem of Calculus
facts.summary: To integrate, find an antiderivative $F$ (so $F' = f$) and subtract its values at the endpoints.

detail.body: The theorem links the two halves of calculus: differentiation and integration undo each other. Evaluate any antiderivative $F$ at $b$ and at $a$, then subtract.

facts.title: Reverse Power Rule (Antiderivative)
facts.summary: Add 1 to the exponent, then divide by the new exponent.

detail.body: This reverses the power rule: where differentiating drops the exponent by 1, integrating raises it by 1 and divides. Don't forget the constant of integration $+C$.
